items.source: Alfonso De Pablos / All Star Charts
items.title: Midterm Seasonality Says We're At The Turn
items.idea: Overlaying every S&P 500 midterm year since 1950 against 2026 YTD, price is tracking the average pattern almost tick for tick — and midterm years have historically bottomed right around mid-July before a stronger H2. All Star Charts pairs that seasonal read with improving market breadth and sector rotation as confirmation, not just a calendar bet.
items.angle: Useful as a sentiment check against Meisler's same-morning breadth warnings — two chart-based sources disagreeing on the market's condition on the same day is itself a signal.
items.tweetA: Every S&P 500 midterm year since 1950 bottoms almost exactly here — mid-July — then rallies into year-end. 2026 is tracking that seasonal curve to the day. The ‘seasonality is dead’ crowd always goes quiet right when it isn’t.
items.tweetB: Two chartists, same morning, opposite read: All Star Charts says seasonality + breadth = buy signal. Meisler's Saturday chart dump says SOX can't clear 12k and insiders just spiked selling. When technicians disagree this hard, that's the tell, not either chart.

charts.desc: S&P 500 average midterm-year path (1950-2022) vs. 2026 YTD overlay — clean visual for the 'we're at the seasonal low' thesis

items.title: Kimi K3 Ships Three Reasoning-Effort Tiers, Same Week As GPT-5.6
items.idea: Kimi K3 now lets users pick Standard/High/Max reasoning effort on web and mobile — landing the same week Sebastian Raschka mapped how six flagship labs (OpenAI, DeepSeek, Nemotron, Kimi, GLM, Inkling) all converged on effort-dial architectures as the default LLM interface.
items.angle: Second independent source today confirming 'effort as a dial' is now table stakes across every major lab within the same week — not a one-off feature.
items.tweetA: Kimi K3 ships Standard/High/Max reasoning tiers the same week Raschka mapped how six different labs independently built the same effort-dial architecture. This isn't a UX trend, it's a shared response to a shared cost problem.
items.tweetB: Every flagship lab converging on low/med/high reasoning modes within months of each other isn't imitation — it's the market pricing test-time compute in real time. Whoever nails auto-effort-selection first (GPT-5's Auto mode flopped) wins the margin war.

charts.desc: Thin paywalled post, no charts in the body

items.title: Coinbase BTC Premium Negative For a Record 60 Straight Days
items.idea: Per Coinglass data cited in the roundup, the Coinbase Bitcoin Premium Index has been negative for 60 consecutive days as of today — a new record beating the prior 40-day streak — with the latest reading at -0.1025%.
items.angle: A 60-day negative US premium during a stalled BTC tape is a cleaner institutional-demand tell than any single ETF flow headline — worth cross-checking against weekly ETF net flows before buying the 'institutions are back' narrative.
items.tweetA: Coinbase premium has been negative for 60 straight days — a new record, beating the old 40-day streak. That's two full months of US buyers paying LESS than the rest of the world for BTC. If institutions were really back, Coinbase would print a premium, not a discount.
items.tweetB: Nobody's tweeting about the Coinbase premium streak because it doesn't fit the 'institutions are accumulating' narrative. 60 days negative is the longest since Coinglass started tracking it. Check this against ETF flows before you believe the flow story.

charts.desc: Data point from a tweet embed, no chart — worth plotting yourself from Coinglass

items.title: PayPal Board Calls Stripe/Advent's $53B Bid 'Inadequate'
items.idea: PayPal's board reportedly views the ~$53B takeover offer from Stripe and Advent as inadequate. A private company (Stripe) is credibly bidding tens of billions for a public payments incumbent, and the board still wants more.
items.angle: Whatever number eventually clears, a rejected $53B bid re-anchors the floor for PYPL far above where the stock currently trades — a private-market mark the public market hasn't caught up to.
items.tweetA: PayPal's board just told Stripe and Advent that $53 billion isn't enough. That's already bigger than Block's entire market cap, rejected as too cheap. Whatever gets this done becomes one of the largest fintech buyouts ever — and the market isn't pricing it. $PYPL
items.tweetB: The real story isn't the rejection, it's that Stripe — still private — is the one setting the price floor on a public payments giant. When private capital outbids public market pricing this aggressively, the public multiple is the one that's wrong.

items.title: SBI Holdings Buys Majority of MAS-Licensed Coinhako
items.idea: Japan's SBI Holdings completed a majority acquisition of Singapore crypto platform Coinhako after getting approval from the Monetary Authority of Singapore — buying an existing regulatory license rather than applying for one.
items.angle: This is the actual TradFi-enters-APAC-crypto playbook: acquire the regulated entity, don't build one from scratch. Watch for more MAS-licensed platforms becoming acquisition targets priced for the license, not just the userbase.
items.tweetA: SBI just bought majority control of a MAS-licensed exchange instead of applying for its own license. That's the real playbook for TradFi entering Asian crypto: buy the regulatory moat, don't build one from zero.
items.tweetB: Every regulated crypto license in Singapore just got more valuable. SBI didn't buy Coinhako's userbase, they bought two years of MAS approval process. That premium doesn't show up in any crypto price chart.

items.title: Grant Cardone Pushes Past 2,700 BTC With Steady DCA
items.idea: Cardone Capital added another 10.5 BTC from July cash flow, a week after adding 107 units, pushing total holdings past 2,700 BTC — explicitly continuing a dollar-cost-average approach despite BTC's weak year-to-date performance.
items.angle: A real-estate-fund operator converting monthly free cash flow into BTC regardless of price action is a small but real data point on non-traditional corporate BTC demand that doesn't show up in MicroStrategy-style headline trackers.
items.tweetA: Cardone Capital just crossed 2,700 BTC, adding steadily every month regardless of price. This isn't a MicroStrategy-style levered bet, it's a real estate fund quietly DCA'ing its free cash flow into BTC through a down year. That's the boring bull case nobody tweets about.
items.tweetB: Real estate fund converts monthly rent roll into Bitcoin, admits BTC 'has not performed well this year,' keeps buying anyway. If DCA discipline through a bad tape doesn't move the price, what does that tell you about who's actually setting BTC's floor right now?

items.title: Andy Burnham Becomes UK PM Unopposed as Gilts Blow Past the Truss Peak
items.idea: Andy Burnham enters No. 10 unopposed this Monday while UK 10-year gilt yields sit at a meaningful premium — in both absolute and relative-to-Treasuries terms — to the 2022 Truss/Kwarteng 'mini-budget' peak. Public sector net borrowing (ex-Covid) is at levels not seen since Brexit.
items.angle: A market pricing UK fiscal risk worse than the literal moment a government nearly triggered a pension-fund LDI crisis is either a screaming buy on excess pessimism or an early warning the market is right this time — worth checking gilt CDS and BoE reaction function before taking a side.
items.tweetA: UK 10-year gilts are yielding MORE, relative to Treasuries, than at the peak of the Truss/Kwarteng mini-budget crisis that broke pension funds in 2022. And this time there's no single villain to blame — just steady-state borrowing at Brexit-era levels. That's the scarier setup.
items.tweetB: Populist theatre and bond market stress hitting the UK simultaneously again, three years after the last blowup, and CME GBP shorts are back at Brexit-era extremes. When the setup looks this similar and yields are already worse than 2022, the trade isn't 'short the pound,' it's 'what did the market miss last time.'

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (gilt yield spread and CME positioning charts referenced in the full post)

items.title: UK Domestic Fund Outflows and Sentiment Hit Post-Brexit-Referendum Extremes
items.idea: BofA's fund manager survey shows UK equity underweights tracking in line with the political chaos period right after the 2016 Brexit referendum, and domestic UK mutual funds saw almost £20 billion in outflows in Q1 alone — the highest of recent years.
items.angle: When positioning and sentiment both sit at decade extremes simultaneously, that's usually closer to a contrarian entry than a reason to keep avoiding the asset class — the author is explicitly using this as the trigger to re-underwrite UK small/mid-caps.
items.tweetA: UK equity underweights are back to 2016-referendum-week extremes. £20B walked out of UK domestic funds in Q1 alone — the worst in years. Everyone already left the trade. That's usually when it stops being a trade worth avoiding.
items.tweetB: The contrarian setup isn't 'UK is cheap,' it's that positioning is now more extreme than it was during the actual Brexit vote itself, while the macro backdrop (borrowing, yields) is arguably worse this time. Either the market has this badly wrong twice in a decade, or small/mid-cap UK is genuinely investable rubble.

items.title: Jane Street Paid Out $9.4B, But Its 30-40 Partners Own a $45B Capital Base Up 2,000% Since 2016
items.idea: Jane Street distributed $9.4B in compensation for 2025 against $39.6B in trading revenue across roughly 3,000 employees — but the firm's members' equity, held by an internal group of just 30-40 people, reached ~$45B, up nearly 2,000% since 2016. A senior researcher clearing $2M participates in the comp pool and owns none of the equity pool.
items.angle: The comp-vs-ownership gap at prop trading firms is the actual reason talent stays a decade-plus: the real wealth compounding happens in an equity ladder that's invisible from outside and rarely discussed even by insiders.
items.tweetA: Jane Street: $9.4B paid to 3,000 employees. Members' equity: $45B, up ~2,000% since 2016, held by roughly 30-40 people. Two completely different games are being played inside the same building, and only one of them shows up on the offer letter.
items.tweetB: The quant comp arms race everyone benchmarks (Citadel vs Optiver vs Jane Street first-year offers) is the wrong ladder to watch. The real one — equity in a capital account compounding 20x in nine years — has maybe three dozen people on it per firm, and it's never posted anywhere.

items.title: Millennium Sold 15% of Itself for $2B — the First Ownership Divestment in 40 Years
items.idea: Millennium sold a 15% minority stake in its management company (via Goldman's Petershill unit and existing institutional investors) for $2B, implying a $14B valuation — the first time in the fund's four-decade history it has sold any piece of itself. Citadel Securities' only external equity was a $1.15B Sequoia/Paradigm check at a $22B valuation in Jan 2022, roughly 5% of the business.
items.angle: Multi-strat platforms monetizing slices of their management companies to outside capital is a structural shift — it converts what used to be a closed compensation ecosystem (deferred comp, profits interests) into something with an actual external mark, which changes every internal negotiation from here on.
items.tweetA: Millennium just sold 15% of itself for $2B — first time in 40 years the firm has sold any piece of the business. Citadel Securities' only outside check was Sequoia/Paradigm's $1.15B at a $22B mark in 2022. Multi-strats spent decades avoiding external valuations. That era just ended.
items.tweetB: Once a platform takes outside capital into the management company, every internal profits-interest and deferred-comp conversation gets anchored to that price forever. Millennium's PMs now know exactly what a 'partner' title is worth in dollars — and it's a number Goldman set, not the founder.

items.source: Sebastian Raschka, PhD / Ahead of AI
items.title: Six Labs, Six Different Recipes for the Same 'Reasoning Effort' Dial
items.idea: Raschka's deep dive maps how DeepSeek V4, Nemotron 3 Ultra, Kimi K2.5, GLM-5, Qwen3, and Inkling each independently implement effort-level control — via separate trained specialists, mode-conditioned RL with different length penalties, hard token budgets, or continuous 0-1 effort values (Inkling) rather than ordinal low/med/high labels. GPT-5.6 ships with 5-6 effort settings across three model sizes (Luna/Terra/Sol).
items.angle: This is the technical substrate behind every 'reasoning effort' UI toggle showing up across labs this week — useful shorthand for explaining to non-technical LPs why inference cost per query is about to become as variable as the query itself.
items.tweetA: Six labs, six different training recipes, same end result: a dial that trades tokens for accuracy. Kimi's 'Toggle' method cuts tokens 25-30% with almost no accuracy loss by alternating budgeted and unconstrained RL phases during training. That's real margin, not a UI gimmick.
items.tweetB: GPT-5's 'Auto' mode — let the model pick its own effort level — got quietly removed because it didn't work well. Every lab still wants automatic effort selection; nobody's cracked it yet. Whoever solves this first has a genuine cost advantage nobody else can copy overnight.

charts.desc: 32 figures total across the piece; the GPT-5.6 effort-vs-cost curve chart (showing model-size curves overlapping with inference-effort curves) is the one worth saving

items.title: Saturday Chart Fest: Lower Highs Everywhere, SOX Stuck at 12k
items.idea: Meisler's weekly technical sweep flags lower highs across almost every major index and sector — SPX, Nasdaq (twice), XLV — with SOX needing to clear 12,000 to avoid 'no bueno,' XLI's CAT needing to hold 175 to avoid leaving an island reversal, and insider selling that spiked three weeks ago only now getting picked up by FinTV.
items.angle: The insider-selling detail is the sleeper here: a signal that was ignored for three weeks only started 'mattering' once price already confirmed it — a reminder that insider data is a leading indicator that the market treats as a lagging one.
items.tweetA: Insider selling spiked three weeks ago. Nobody cared. Now that tech has actually rolled over, FinTV is suddenly talking about it. Price didn't confirm the signal — price is why people finally noticed the signal existed.
items.tweetB: SOX at 12,000 is the whole tech tape in one level right now. Clear it, the 'AI trade is fine' story holds. Fail it a second time and you've got a confirmed lower high on the single sector most levered to the AI capex story. Binary week ahead. $SOXX

charts.desc: Roughly 30 individual ticker charts (Dow, SPX, SOX, XLE, GLD, DXY, McClellan Summation, Citi Panic/Euphoria, etc.) — worth opening for the full technical picture

items.title: The 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act Just Passed — a YIMBY Bill Trump Refused to Sign but Couldn't Stop
items.idea: A bipartisan housing bill passed the House and Senate with overwhelming majorities and became law despite Trump refusing to sign it — expedited environmental review for housing, more federal funding tied to housing production, and deregulation of prefab construction and housing finance. It also contains a heavily carved-out restriction on corporate single-family landlords, based on a myth Smith says the data doesn't support since corporate landlords are a tiny sliver of the market and may even lower rents.
items.angle: A veto-proof, deregulatory, supply-side housing bill clearing a maximally polarized Congress validates the 'Secret Congress' theory that unglamorous, low-profile bipartisan wins are still possible — worth tracking for downstream effects on homebuilder and prefab-construction names.
items.tweetA: Congress just passed a genuinely deregulatory, supply-side housing bill — faster environmental review, more federal money for building more, prefab finance deregulation — with veto-proof bipartisan majorities, and Trump couldn't even block it by refusing to sign. Nobody's pricing what actually clears the pipeline in 18 months because of this.
items.tweetB: The corporate-landlord provision made headlines. It's also functionally toothless — so many carve-outs that build-to-rent operators are explicitly excluded. Classic 'give the populists a symbolic win, give supply-siders the actual policy' bill. Watch prefab and modular construction names, not the landlord narrative.

items.title: Noah Smith's Spanish Civil War Analogy for 2026 America
items.idea: Smith argues America's three dominant political factions — MAGA right, socialist left, 'woke' left — mirror the fractured, mutually hostile factions of 1930s Spain in that none has a workable governing program, and the 1934 leftist revolt that panicked the Spanish right into backing Franco parallels how 2020's unrest hardened right-wing authoritarian sentiment in the US.
items.angle: Whether or not the analogy holds militarily (Smith says it won't turn into an actual civil war), the framework is a useful lens for why 'big, bold' US policy (FDR/Reagan scale) looks structurally foreclosed for years, while narrow bipartisan wins (like the housing bill above) remain the only viable channel.
items.tweetA: Noah Smith's read on 2026 US politics: three factions, none with a workable governing program, each held together mostly by fear of the other two. That's not a hot take, that's a description of Spain in 1936 before the shooting started — he's explicit the US won't get the shooting part, but the paralysis part is already here.
items.tweetB: The actual investable takeaway buried in a political-economy essay: 'Secret Congress' — quiet, low-profile bipartisan bills — is now the ONLY mechanism left for policy to move. Anything visible enough to become a culture-war flashpoint is dead on arrival. Screen for legislation flying under the radar, not headline fights.

items.title: Warsh's 'Non-Exclusive List': Balance-Sheet Tools, Not Rate Cuts, Are the Real September Lever
items.idea: Knapp argues the Fed is over-relying on chain-weighted PCE (PCED) inflation data distorted by technology and seasonal-adjustment quirks, while CPI/PPI show broad disinflation. He frames Kevin Warsh's 'non-exclusive list' as favoring balance-sheet tools — ending reserve-management purchases, shortening SOMA duration — over a surprise rate move to address AI-driven capital demand, with September flagged as the likely inflection for policy rebalancing.
items.angle: If the Fed's actual lever in September is balance-sheet mechanics rather than the fed funds rate, the market narrative fixated on 'will they cut' may be watching the wrong dial entirely — worth tracking SOMA duration and reserve-purchase pace, not just the dot plot.
items.tweetA: The framing everyone's missing: the Fed's September move might not be a rate cut or hike at all. It's balance-sheet mechanics — ending reserve-management purchases, shortening SOMA duration — aimed specifically at AI-driven capital demand. Nobody's pricing balance-sheet policy risk into rate-sensitive trades.
items.tweetB: CPI says disinflation. The Fed's preferred gauge, PCED, says something messier because of tech and seasonal-adjustment noise. When the data series the Fed actually targets is the one with the dirtier read, that's not a policy debate, that's a measurement problem driving policy.

charts.desc: Paywalled — chartbook referenced but not visible in preview

items.title: Hydrocarbons Still 86.24% of Global Primary Energy — Down From 86.65%
items.idea: The Energy Institute's 2026 Statistical Review shows hydrocarbons provided 86.24% of global primary energy in 2025, barely down from 86.65% the year before, even as every major hydrocarbon set a fresh consumption record. Renewables captured 'a third of global energy growth' in 2025 — technically true, but total primary energy only grew ~1%, so a third of that is a rounding error.
items.angle: The 'a third of growth' framing is a share-of-a-tiny-denominator trick that reads as a big transition headline but nets out to almost nothing in absolute terms — a good gut-check anytime an ESG or transition narrative cites a growth-share stat instead of an absolute one.
items.tweetA: Hydrocarbons: 86.24% of global primary energy in 2025. A year earlier: 86.65%. That's the entire global energy transition in one number — 41 basis points in twelve months, after a decade of trillion-dollar headlines. Do the math before you buy the narrative.
items.tweetB: 'Renewables captured a third of global energy growth' sounds like a big transition story until you check that total energy demand only grew ~1% that year. A third of almost nothing is still almost nothing. Always ask growth-share stats what the denominator actually was.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (Doomberg typically includes a hydrocarbon-share time series)

items.title: China: 56% of World Coal Consumption, 31% of Carbon Emissions vs. US 5%/13%
items.idea: Doomberg flags that China accounts for 56% of global coal consumption and 31% of all carbon emissions, versus the US at 5% and 13% respectively — while the same Energy Institute report frames China as 'deploying renewables at unprecedented scale' and notes the increase in US emissions was '4x greater' than China's, despite China's vastly larger absolute footprint.
items.angle: The emissions-growth framing (US grew 4x more than China) obscures the absolute base — China's coal and carbon share dwarfs the US on any denominator that matters for actual climate math, which matters for how you read any China-decarbonization-driven commodity or solar-supply-chain thesis.
items.tweetA: China: 56% of world coal consumption, 31% of global carbon emissions. US: 5% and 13%. Same report says US emissions grew '4x greater in absolute terms' than China's — true, and also the kind of stat that makes a much bigger number look smaller by comparison.
items.tweetB: If you're trading the China-solar or China-decarbonization story, know the base rate: Beijing burns more than half the world's coal while being cast as the renewables hero of the same report. The stock story and the emissions story are not the same story.

items.source: Stephen Innes / The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Treasury's New Owners Are Price-Sensitive — and They Want a Bigger Term Premium
items.idea: Citing UBS economist Arend Kapteyn, Innes lays out how Treasury demand hasn't disappeared but has shifted ownership — from the Fed and foreign central banks toward mutual funds, ETFs, money-market funds (household savings) and leveraged, repo-dependent hedge funds. Greater reliance on bills makes debt easier to place short-term but shortens Washington's refinancing clock and raises rate-policy exposure.
items.angle: The risk isn't a buyers' strike, it's that every new marginal buyer in this ownership mix demands a higher term premium than the central-bank buyer it replaced — a structural, not cyclical, upward pressure on the cost of US government borrowing that compounds every rollover.
items.tweetA: The Treasury market didn't lose buyers, it changed buyers. Central banks out, mutual funds/ETFs/hedge funds in. Every one of those new owners wants a bigger term premium than the old one did. That's not a one-time repricing, that's a permanent tax on every future rollover.
items.tweetB: Washington leaning harder into bills makes today's auctions easier to clear and tomorrow's refinancing clock faster. It's the fiscal equivalent of switching from a 30-year mortgage to a stack of 90-day loans because the rate looks better this quarter. Convenient until the roll comes due in a stress event.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (UBS ownership-composition chart referenced but paywalled)
